title: Soil Health Comparison: Tillage Methods
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive MicroSim comparing the long-term effects of conventional tillage vs. no-till farming on soil health indicators over 20 simulated years.

learningObjectives: Evaluate the long-term effects of conventional tillage vs. no-till farming on soil health indicators
Analyze how farming practices affect soil organic carbon, water infiltration, erosion, and biodiversity
Assess the trade-offs between short-term agricultural productivity and long-term soil sustainability
Propose evidence-based farming recommendations that balance yield with soil conservation
prerequisites: Understanding of soil horizons and basic soil properties
Familiarity with sustainable vs. unsustainable resource use
Basic knowledge of agriculture and food production

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Time-lapse side-by-side comparison with quantitative metrics
keyQuestions: How does soil organic carbon change over 20 years under each tillage method?
Why does water infiltration decrease with conventional tillage?
Is no-till farming always better, or are there trade-offs?
How do soil health practices connect to climate change and water quality?
Why might a farmer resist switching to no-till despite long-term benefits?
commonMisconceptions: Tilling soil is always necessary for good crop growth
Soil health changes are visible within a single growing season rather than over decades
No-till farming always produces lower yields than conventional tillage
assessmentOpportunities: Students compare metrics at year 5 vs. year 20 to evaluate short-term vs. long-term outcomes
Students write a recommendation letter to a farmer using simulation data as evidence
Students connect soil health metrics to broader environmental impacts
